coredata.dc:title: Interface modulation of BiVO4 based photoanode with Bi(III)Bi(V)O4 for enhanced solar water splitting
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Ming
Xing, Zheng
Zhu, Xi
Jiang, Peng
Wang, Xiao
Lin, He
An, Yiming
Su, Haibin
Yang, Shihe
coredata.dc:description: Despite being considered as one of the most promising semiconductor photocatalysts, BiVO4 still suffers the problems of inefficient light harvesting, multiple charge recombination channels and back reactions, restricting its application for solar energy conversion. Here, we demonstrate a unique Bi2O4(400)/BiVO4(040) heterojunction prepared via an oxidation conversion process, which dramatically accelerated the interfacial charge transfer compared to pure BiVO4. Furthermore, with Mo doping, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) loading and Ni-FeOOH co-catalyst deposition, the resulting Bi2O4/Mo-BiVO4/CQDs/Ni-FeOOH photoanode reaches a remarkable photocurrent density of 6.7 mA/cm2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE under AM 1.5G irradiation in the absence of hole scavengers. Our findings demonstrate that proper material interface engineering together with composition tuning provides a viable route to achieve highly efficient solar water splitting.
coredata.dcterms:subject: BiVO4
Bi2O4
Oxidation conversion
Interface modulation
Solar water splitting

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram of the fabrication process for Bi2O4/BiVO4 sample. SEM images of 10 cycle BiOCl/BiVO4 sample with different light illumination time as (b) 0 min, (c) 2 min, (d) 5 min, (e) 10 min, (f) 30 min and (g) 60 min.

TEM images of (a) BiVO4 and (b) 5 min-Bi2O4/BiVO4 sample. (c) HR-TEM image of 5 min-Bi2O4/BiVO4 sample. (d) The atomic structure of the Bi2O4/BiVO4 heterojunction, the black dash line indicates the interface between the two phases. (e) PDOS for the Bi and V atoms in BiVO4 and Bi2O4. (f) Bi 4f XPS spectra of Mo-BiVO4 and 5 min-Bi2O4/Mo-BiVO4 samples with the de-convoluted curves for the rear one. (g) O 1s XPS spectra of Mo-BiVO4 and 5 min-Bi2O4/Mo-BiVO4 samples.

LSV curves of (a) different cycles BiOCl deposited on BiVO4 samples with certain 10 min light illumination treatment and (b) LSV curves of 10 cycles BiOCl/BiVO4 samples with different light illumination treatment time.

(a) LSV curves and (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of Mo-BiVO4 and 5 min-Bi2O4/Mo-BiVO4. (c) LSV curves of Mo-BiVO4/Ni-FeOOH and 5 min-Bi2O4/Mo-BiVO4/Ni-FeOOH. (d-f) XPS spectra of Mo 3d, Ni 2p and Fe 2p for 5 min-Bi2O4/Mo-BiVO4/Ni-FeOOH sample. (g) LSV curves and (h) UV–vis absorption spectra of 5 min-Bi2O4/Mo-BiVO4 and 5 min-Bi2O4/Mo-BiVO4/CQDs. (i) LSV curves of 5 min-Bi2O4/Mo-BiVO4/Ni-FeOOH and 5 min-Bi2O4/Mo-BiVO4/CQDs/Ni-FeOOH.
KPFM images in dark and under illumination of (a) Mo-BiVO4 and (b) 5 min-Bi2O4/Mo-BiVO4. Potential distributions obtained from the KPFM images of (c) Mo-BiVO4 and (d) 5 min-Bi2O4/Mo-BiVO4.

Mott-Schottky plots of (a) Mo-BiVO4 and (b) 5 min-Bi2O4/Mo-BiVO4 samples. (c) UPS spectra of the Mo-BiVO4 and 5 min-Bi2O4/Mo-BiVO4 samples. (d) Energy band diagram of the Mo-BiVO4 and 5 min-Bi2O4/Mo-BiVO4 samples.

head.ce:title: Interface modulation of BiVO4 based photoanode with Bi(III)Bi(V)O4 for enhanced solar water splitting

Su). Department of Chemistry, The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology, Clear Water Bay, Kowloon, Hong Kong, China (S. Yang)

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Key Lab of Nano-Micro Material Research
School of Chemical Biology and Biotechnology

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bi2O4(400)/BiVO4(040) heterojunction has been designed through a novel oxidation conversion progress with facilitated interfacial charge transfer. The optimized Bi2O4/Mo-BiVO4/CQDs/Ni-FeOOH photoanode reached a remarkable photocurrent of 6.7 mA/cm2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE under AM 1.5G without using any hole scavenger.

Despite being considered as one of the most promising semiconductor photocatalysts, BiVO4 still suffers the problems of inefficient light harvesting, multiple charge recombination channels and back reactions, restricting its application for solar energy conversion. Here, we demonstrate a unique Bi2O4(400)/BiVO4(040) heterojunction prepared via an oxidation conversion process, which dramatically accelerated the interfacial charge transfer compared to pure BiVO4. Furthermore, with Mo doping, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) loading and Ni-FeOOH co-catalyst deposition, the resulting Bi2O4/Mo-BiVO4/CQDs/Ni-FeOOH photoanode reaches a remarkable photocurrent density of 6.7 mA/cm2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE under AM 1.5G irradiation in the absence of hole scavengers. Our findings demonstrate that proper material interface engineering together with composition tuning provides a viable route to achieve highly efficient solar water splitting.

ce:section.ce:para: For almost five decades, numerous efforts have been devoted to the developing of solar water splitting machineries to produce hydrogen as an ideal energy carrier [1,2]. However, the overall solar water splitting efficiency is often limited by the oxidation process, which is largely dependent on the physiochemical properties of photoanode materials [3]. Bismuth vanadate (BiVO4) with monoclinic scheelite structure is one of the most studied sustainable photoanode materials due to its relatively small bandgap (around 2.4 eV) and suitable valence band edge position [4], but its PEC performance is still greatly hindered by the severe charge recombination and slow surface catalytic kinetics [5–7]. In this regard, finding a viable strategy to facilitate charge transport, especially at the interfaces, is of critical importance.

Chemical modification has been exploited to solve the abovementioned problems of BiVO4. For example, a black BiVO4@amorphous TiO2-x photoanode, fabricated through atomic layer deposition and subsequent hydrogen plasma treatment, exhibited enhanced surface reaction kinetics and promoted photostability due to the introduction of the TiO2-x outer layer [8]. Meng et al. soaked a BiVO4 photoanode in borate buffer solution to modify the local catalytic environment with borate layer as both passivator and co-catalyst, which achieved a photocurrent density of 3.5 mA cm−2 at 1.23 V and a cathodically shifted onset potential of 250 mV [9]. Although those surface modification trials minimized the interfacial charge transfer barrier, the approach was accompanied by the surface screening with foreign materials, which restricted the full potential of the BiVO4 photoanode materials and also brought in additional interfacial barrier to some extent.

Recently, a bismuth-based metal oxide, Bi2O4, with mixed valence states (Bi3+ and Bi5+), has been utilized for photocatalytic reactions, such as organic pollutants degradation, sterilization and carbon dioxide reduction [10–13]. Having a compatible crystal structure with BiVO4 [14,15], Bi2O4 is potential material to form a coherent heterojunction with BiVO4. In this work, we venture to explore an oxidation conversion method to top Bi2O4 nanoparticles closely on the surface of BiVO4, as shown in Fig. 1 a, with the aim to facilitate interfacial charge transport through the well-aligned lattice docking junction. The BiVO4 photoanode is further optimized via Mo doping and co-loading with CQDs as light absorber and Ni-FeOOH as co-catalyst. While the pristine BiVO4 photoanode only produces a photocurrent of 1.3 mA/cm2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE under AM 1.5G irradiation, the optimized Bi2O4/Mo-BiVO4 junction photoanode can achieve 2.7 mA/cm2 under the same conditions, and with further modification, the obtained Bi2O4/Mo-BiVO4/CQDs/Ni-FeOOH raises the value to, astonishingly, 6.7 mA/cm2.

The pristine BiVO4 photoelectrode was obtained via conversion from a BiOI film prepared via electro-deposition [16], with morphologies shown in Fig. S1. The porous BiVO4 structure consisted of small particles with uniform sizes could be observed from the SEM images. To deposit Bi2O4 on the surface of BiVO4, BiOCl nanosheets were first grown on BiVO4 by a chemical batch deposition method. The details could be found in the experimental section. The amount of BiOCl could be controlled by adjusting the number of deposition cycles, with the obtained samples named as X cycles BiOCl/BiVO4 (X: 5, 10, 20 and 40), as shown in Fig. S2. With the increase of deposition cycles, BiOCl nanosheets gradually appeared on the surface of BiVO4 particles. It can be seen from the cross-sectional SEM images that the deposition of BiOCl did not cause any obvious change to the overall thicknesses of the BiVO4 films, indicating the growth of BiOCl in the pores of the BiVO4 films. Afterwards, the BiOCl nanosheets were converted to Bi2O4 particles by exposing the BiOCl/BiVO4 samples to light illumination in the presence of NaOH solution. The X cycles BiOCl/BiVO4 samples were irradiated for 10 min and named as X-Bi2O4-BiVO4 (X: 5, 10, 20 and 40). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were collected for these samples (Fig. S3), and typical peaks of monoclinic BiVO4 (JCPDS No. 14-0688) were always existing [17]. Tetragonal BiOCl phase (JCPDS No. 06-0249) [18] appeared for 20-Bi2O4/BiVO4 and 40-Bi2O4/BiVO4, but were absent in 5-Bi2O4/BiVO4 and 10-Bi2O4/BiVO4, indicative of complete conversion of BiOCl nanosheets. In addition, a peak of Bi2O4 can be found at around 27.3°, which could be indexed to the (111) facet of monoclinic Bi2O4 (JCPDS No. 50-0846).

As a photoanode, 10-Bi2O4-BiVO4 showed the optimum PEC performance among all X-Bi2O4-BiVO4 samples, which will be described in detail thereinafter. In order to optimize the Bi2O4 deposition, different illumination times of 2, 5, 10, 30 and 60 min were tested for the 10 cycles BiOCl/BiVO4 sample, with morphologies shown in Fig. 1. The samples were named as Ymin-Bi2O4/BiVO4 (Y: 2, 5, 10, 30 and 60). For the original sample (Fig. 1b), the BiOCl nanosheets can be observed on top of the BiVO4 particles, which became smaller after 2 min illumination (Fig. 1c) and almost disappeared when the treatment time increased to 5 min (Fig. 1d). The emerged smaller particles with the consumption of BiOCl nanosheets should be the newly formed Bi2O4. Prolonged irradiation caused formation of even more particles (Fig. 1f and g), which should not be Bi2O4 considering that all the BiOCl nanosheets were almost already consumed. In a control experiment, pure BiVO4 was treated under the same conditions, and the similar small particles were observed after illumination (Fig. S4), which became more obvious after extended reaction, possibly due to the decomposition of BiVO4. Therefore, it is surmised that the BiVO4 decomposition tends to occur after all the BiOCl nanosheets are consumed to form Bi2O4 particles. It has been reported previously that the introduction of Mo6+ to partially replace V5+ in BiVO4 could alter its crystal symmetry with the attendant formation of electron polarons, resulting in a high charge carrier density [19–21]. Thus, different concentrations of Mo dopants (1%, 2% and 3% mol) were introduced to the BiVO4 photoanode, and it was found that 2% Mo doping showed as the best condition (discussed below). For convenience, we used Mo-BiVO4 and 5 min-Bi2O4/Mo-BiVO4 as the abbreviations of the 2% Mo doped BiVO4 samples. Moreover, the incorporation of Mo dopants did not induce any obvious morphology change, as confirmed by SEM (Fig. S5).

In order to observe the formation of Bi2O4 nanoparticles and the interfacial structure, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurement was conducted and the images are provided in Fig. 2 . By comparing the morphologies of BiVO4 (Fig. 2a) and 5 min-Bi2O4/BiVO4 (Fig. 2b), Bi2O4 nanoparticles formed on the surface of BiVO4 for the 5 min-Bi2O4/BiVO4 sample. More TEM images can be found in Fig. S6, showing a uniform distribution of the Bi2O4 particles on the surface of BiVO4. When viewed up close in the HR (high resolution)-TEM image (Fig. 2c), the BiVO4-Bi2O4 junction showed a well-aligned structure. The smooth transition from the (400) and (021) crystal planes of Bi2O4 to the (040) crystal plane of BiVO4 clearly suggested a lattice docking junction between the Bi2O4 and BiVO4 particles, which facilitates the holes migration from BiVO4, possibly through the (110) plane, to Bi2O4 [22–24]. Theoretical simulation has been performed to gain more insight to the way how Bi2O4 and BiVO4 are joined together. As shown in Fig. 2d, in the crystal structure of Bi(III)Bi(V)O4, Bi3+ and Bi5+ are associated with the purple (Bi) and green (Bi*) colored polyhedrons, respectively. The purple Bi polyhedrons can act as the bridge to connect BiVO4 and BiBiO4, with the Bi-O bond lengths calculated to be 2.42 Å and 2.44 Å. In addition, the similar PDOS (partial density of states) characteristics of the purple colored Bi atoms in both BiVO4 and BiBiO4 indicate proper electronic structure matching at the interface (Fig. 2e).

To verify the lattice docking junction, the surface chemical states were examined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) for the 5 min-Bi2O4/Mo-BiVO4 sample, with Mo-BiVO4 measured as a control. From the overlapped Bi 4f XPS spectra (Fig. S7), the Bi 4f peaks showed a shift to higher binding energy with the addition of Bi2O4 particles, which should be resulted from Bi5+. In Fig. 2f, the Bi 4f peaks of 5 min-Bi2O4/Mo-BiVO4 can be deconvoluted into two typical pairs of bismuth oxidation states: Bi3+ (centered at 164.7 and 159.4 eV) and Bi5+ (centered at 165.3 and 159.9 eV) [25,26]. For the Mo-BiVO4 sample, the Bi 4f XPS peaks could be well assigned to Bi3+. Thus, the shift to higher binding energy of the Bi 4f peaks was caused by the Bi5+ from Bi2O4, illustrating the successful formation of Bi2O4. The O 1s XPS spectra were also measured and presented in Fig. 2g. The peaks at around 530.2 eV could be ascribed to the lattice oxygen in BiVO4 [27], and the positions and intensities of these peaks for Mo-BiVO4 and 5 min-Bi2O4/Mo-BiVO4 were almost the same. The O 1s peak at around 532 eV assigned to the non-uniform surface sites (such as dangling oxygen or hydroxyl group) [28,29] was observed for the Mo-BiVO4 sample. However, the formation of Bi2O4 particles on the surface of Mo-BiVO4 significantly weakened this O 1s XPS signal. The dramatically reduced dangle oxygen moieties may eliminate the PEC over-potential induced by dangling oxygen [30]. In addition, V 2p XPS spectra were measured and collected for comparison in Fig. S8, showing slightly decreased intensities of V 2p signal peaks due to the coverage of Bi2O4 particles.

The PEC evaluation results of the X-Bi2O4-BiVO4 (X: 5, 10, 20 and 40) samples were shown in Fig. 3 a. As can be seen from the linear sweep voltammograms (LSVs), 10-Bi2O4/BiVO4 and 20-Bi2O4/BiVO4 showed improved photocurrent compared to BiVO4 and negatively shifted onset potential was present for all the treated samples. 5-Bi2O4/BiVO4 produced slightly lower photocurrent at low potentials compared to the other treated samples, possibly due to the decomposition of BiVO4 particles. The photocurrents measured after treatment time being adjusted to 2, 5, 10, 30 and 60 min are shown in Fig. 3b. As can be seen from the J-V curves, 2 min-Bi2O4/BiVO4 showed no superior PEC performance compared to pure BiVO4, likely due to the remaining BiOCl after treatment. For reference, the optical properties of BiVO4 and 10 cycles BiOCl/BiVO4 were measured (Fig. S9), and 10 cycles BiOCl made almost no contribution to the light absorption for BiOCl/BiVO4. The remaining BiOCl may affect the charge transport between Bi2O4 and BiVO4, leading to minimal PEC performance boost. With the increase of treatment time, as for the 5 min-Bi2O4/BiVO4 and 10 min-Bi2O4/BiVO4 samples, the photocurrents were hugely enhanced and onset potential shifted more. While BiVO4 only generated 1.3 mA/cm2 photocurrent at 1.23 V vs. RHE, 5 min-Bi2O4/BiVO4 can produce 1.9 mA/cm2 with 50% improvement. 10 min-Bi2O4/BiVO4 showed slightly lower activity compared to 5 min-Bi2O4/BiVO4 due to the partial decomposition of BiVO4. The decomposition of BiVO4 after treatment has been demonstrated from the morphologies (Fig. S4) with the PEC performance shown in Fig. S10. The 30 min-Bi2O4/BiVO4 and 60 min-Bi2O4/BiVO4 samples exhibited similar PEC activities compared to pure BiVO4 under the same treatment conditions. Therefore, 5 min of illumination not only converted all the 10 cycles BiOCl to Bi2O4, but also led to the highest catalytic ability for solar water oxidation. Meanwhile, as shown in Fig. S11, Bi2O4 particles on BiVO4 enhanced the light absorption, which may be one of the reasons for the promoted photoactivity. In addition, the influence of different intermediates formed in the synthesis of Bi2O4 particles has been investigated under similar synthesis conditions, including BiOI and BiOBr. The samples fabricated through BiOI and BiOBr showed inferior PEC performance than that through BiOCl, as shown in Fig. S12, which may be ascribed to the change of Bi2O4 structure and the connection between Bi2O4 and BiVO4. Therefore, BiOCl was used for the Bi2O4 deposition in this work.

After the introduction of Mo dopants, LSV curves of Mo-BiVO4 samples were collected. As shown in Fig. S13, 2% Mo-BiVO4 generated the highest photocurrent of 1.7 mA/cm2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE. For convenience, 2% Mo-BiVO4 will be shortened as Mo-BiVO4. Bi2O4 was fabricated on the surface of Mo-BiVO4 under the optimal conditions described above (5 min treatment with 10 cycles BiOCl deposition), and the photocurrent of 5 min-Bi2O4/Mo-BiVO4 reached 2.7 mA/cm2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE (Fig. 4 a). It is found that the introduction of Bi2O4 enhanced the absorption of Mo-BiVO4 (Fig. 4b and S14). In addition, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out at 1.23 V vs. RHE with solar light illumination (Fig. S15), illustrating enhanced conductivity after the addition of Bi2O4 particles and better charge transport between BiVO4 and Bi2O4.

In previous studies, oxygen evolution catalysts (OECs), such as Co-Pi, FeOOH, NiOOH and CoOOH have been deposited on the surface of BiVO4 photoanode to improve the surface kinetics and increase the utilization efficiency of the surface photo-holes [16,31,32]. In this respect, the bi-layered Ni-FeOOH OEC layer has been reported as the best co-catalyst for BiVO4 to date [25]. Therefore, a Ni-FeOOH OEC layer was deposited to Mo-BiVO4 and 5 min-Bi2O4/Mo-BiVO4. The photocurrent was further improved to 5.1 mA/cm2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE for 5 min-Bi2O4/Mo-BiVO4/Ni-FeOOH (Fig. 4c). The XPS spectra of Mo 3d, Ni 2p and Fe 2p of this sample (Fig. 4d-f) verified the successful doping of Mo element and the successful deposition of the Ni-FeOOH layer. In addition, as CQDs have been proved to enhance the light absorption and surface charge transport of BiVO4 [33], we also loaded CQDs onto 5 min-Bi2O4/Mo-BiVO4. It is found that 5 min-Bi2O4/Mo-BiVO4/CQDs possessed both improved light absorption (Fig. 4h and S16) and more importantly elevated photocurrent of around 3.2 mA/cm2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE (Fig. 4g). Incident photo to current conversion efficiencies (IPCE) were measured at 1.23 V vs. RHE under simulated solar light illumination (Fig. S17) for Mo-BiVO4, 5 min-Bi2O4/Mo-BiVO4 and 5 min-Bi2O4/Mo-BiVO4/CQDs, illustrating remarkably enhanced solar light utilization (extending the absorption range to beyond 600 nm) after the addition of CQDs.

When Ni-FeOOH co-catalyst layer and CQDs were simultaneously introduced, the photocurrent was drastically boosted to 6.7 mA/cm2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE for the 5 min-Bi2O4/Mo-BiVO4/CQDs/Ni-FeOOH sample (Fig. 4i), which remained very stable up to 10 hrs (Fig. S18). The applied bias photo-to-current efficiency (ABPE) values were calculated from the I-V curves assuming 100% faradaic efficiency, as shown in Fig. S19, and it can reach 2.35% at 0.70 V vs. RHE.

It has been reported that the charge transport (separation) and transfer efficiencies could be obtained photoelectrochemically via measuring the photocurrents with hole scavengers, according to the following equations [34–36]:
(1) JH 2 O = Jmax × ηabs × ηsep × ηtrans
(2) Jsul = Jmax × ηabs × ηsep
(3) ηsep = Jsul / (Jmax × ηabs)
(4) ηtrans = JH2O / Jsul

Here JH2O
J
H 2
O
is the recorded water oxidation photocurrent density, Jsul is the recorded Na2SO3 (hole scavenger) oxidation photocurrent density, Jmax is the maximum photocurrent density given that the IPCE for photons with energy ≥ Eg is 100%, ηabs is the light absorption efficiency, ηsep is the charge separation efficiency and ηtrans is the surface transfer efficiency.

We have also tried to measure charge separation efficiencies via PEC techniques. However, under illumination, the Bi2O4/Mo-BiVO4 sample reacted with the hole scavengers (as Na2SO3, H2O2 and methanol) and black particles came out on surface of the sample. Therefore, the exact charge transfer and charge transport efficiencies could not be obtained via the PEC technique. To gain insight into the charge separation in the Bi2O4/Mo-BiVO4 heterojunction, in-situ Kevin-probe force microscopy (KPFM) was exploited and the surface potential changes upon illumination for both Mo-BiVO4 and 5 min-Bi2O4/Mo-BiVO4 have been monitored. KPFM coupled with irradiation sources allowed the imaging of photogenerated charge carriers in semiconductors by measuring the contact potential difference (CPD) between the scanning probe and material surface [37–39]. As shown in Fig. 5 a and 5b, both Mo-BiVO4 and 5 min-Bi2O4/Mo-BiVO4 exhibited negative CPD shift upon illumination by a laser source, as reflected by the darkened color in the images. The negative shift was attributed to the accumulation of electrons at the sample surface [40]. 5 min-Bi2O4/Mo-BiVO4 showed an average potential drop of 130 mV (Fig. 5d), which was significantly larger than that of Mo-BiVO4 (31 mV, Fig. 5c), clearly evidencing the boosted electron build-up at the surface of 5 min-Bi2O4/Mo-BiVO4 and thus improved charge separation in the heterojunction.

In order to corroborate the thermodynamic possibility of lowering the onset potential for solar water oxidation after the addition of Bi2O4, Mott-Schottky plots were drew and compared for Mo-BiVO4 and 5 min-Bi2O4/Mo-BiVO4 (Fig. 6 a and b). The flat band potential (Efb) of 5 min-Bi2O4/Mo-BiVO4 extracted from the x-axis intercept of the Mott-Schottky plots was negatively-shifted compared to that of Mo-BiVO4. The negatively shifted flat band potential would provide higher quasi-Fermi level of electrons that could drive greater separation with the quasi-Fermi level of holes, facilitating the surface charge transfer efficiency [25].

The ultraviolet photoelectron spectra (UPS) were measured to gain information about the valence bands of Mo-BiVO4 and 5 min-Bi2O4/Mo-BiVO4 (Fig. 6c and S20). With the employment of ultraviolet photons, UPS can probe the electrons in the outermost valence levels of the materials, providing the surface electronic structural information, such as the valence band maximum (VBM) [41]. In Fig. 6c, linear extrapolation was used to estimate the VBM for the Mo-BiVO4 and 5 min-Bi2O4/Mo-BiVO4 samples, showing upward shift of the valence band position after the addition of Bi2O4. From the Tauc plots (Fig. S21), 5 min-Bi2O4/Mo-BiVO4 possessed a smaller band gap (~2.44 eV) compared to Mo-BiVO4 (~2.47 eV). Based on flat band potential, VBM and band gap values, a qualitative energy level diagram for Mo-BiVO4 and 5 min-Bi2O4/Mo-BiVO4 could be drawn to demonstrate the influence of Bi2O4 (Fig. 6d). It can be concluded that the negatively shifted band positions of 5 min-Bi2O4/Mo-BiVO4 and flat band potentials should reflect the facilitated charge transport between BiVO4 and Bi2O4 particles.

In summary, when solar light was irradiated on the 5 min-Bi2O4/Mo-BiVO4/CQDs/Ni-FeOOH photoanode, the electron-hole pairs were generated in Bi2O4, BiVO4 and CQDs. In this process, the lattice docking junction of Bi2O4/BiVO4 could facilitate the electron-hole separation and the migration of electrons from Bi2O4 to BiVO4, leaving holes behind to the surface sites of Bi2O4. In the meantime, the CQDs served to enhance the ability to generate more electrons and holes due to their widened light harvesting wavelength range. Moreover, at the outer layer, the Ni-FeOOH co-catalyst as acted to promote the hole transport and the final water oxidation.

In this work, we have developed a series of strategies to improve the activities of BiVO4 photoanode for solar water oxidation, e.g., by promoting interfacial charge diffusion, surface charge transfer and light absorption range. Most notably, the incorporation of Bi2O4 nanoparticles to construct a smooth lattice-docking heterojunction through the oxidation conversion method has been demonstrated to be crucial for improving the PEC performance of BiVO4. With the improved charge separation efficiency, Bi2O4/BiVO4 presents a promising anode for PEC cells. The ABPE value of the optimized sample with addition of CQDs and Ni-FeOOH co-catalyst could achieve 2.35% at 0.70 V vs. RHE. Our heterojunction system represents an exemplar for developing novel PEC photoelectrodes.

Electro-deposition of BiOI films: The Bi(NO3)3 solution was prepared by dissolving 0.97 g Bi(NO3)3·5H2O (Sigma-Aldrich, 98%) and 3.32 g KI (Sigma-Aldrich, ≥99.0%) in 50 mL deionized (DI) water with vigorous stirring. Then, the solution pH was adjusted to 1.7 with adding 2.0 M HNO3 solution. Afterwards, 20 mL ethanol solution with 0.4972 g dissolved p-benzoquinone (Sigma-Aldrich, ≥98%) was mixed with the previous Bi(NO3)3 solution with stirring for 30 min. Electro-deposition of BiOI films was conducted in a typical three-electrode cell, with FTO (fluorine-doped tin oxide, 1 cm * 2 cm) as the working electrode, Ag/AgCl (saturated KCl) as the reference electrode and platinum foil as the counter electrode. The deposition process was performed at −0.1 V vs. Ag/AgCl with 150 s deposition time.

Preparation of BiVO4 films: Vanadyl acetylacetonate (VO(acac)2, Sigma-Aldrich, 98%) was dissolved in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, Sigma-Aldrich, ≥99.5%) with 0.2 M concentration. 90 μl solution was dropped on the BiOI films (1 cm * 2 cm) with following heated in a furnace at 450 °C for 2 h in air. Then the BiVO4 films could be obtained by soaking in 1 M NaOH solution for 30 min with stirring to remove the excess V2O5. Finally, the sample should be cleaned by DI water and dried at room temperature. For the fabrication of Mo-BiVO4, a mixture of VO(acac)2 and Molybdenum acetylacetonate (MoO2(acac)2, Sigma-Aldrich) was dissolved in DMSO solvent instead of pure VO(acac)2 with 0.2 M concentration. The mixture mole ratio of Mo:V was set as 1:100 (for 1% Mo-BiVO4), 2:100 (for 2% Mo-BiVO4) and 3:100 (for 3% Mo-BiVO4). The subsequent annealing process was consistent with that for BiVO4 fabrication.

Synthesis of BiOCl nanosheets on BiVO4 films: The BiOCl nanosheets were synthesized on surface of BiVO4 films through chemical batch deposition process. In order to realize the formation of BiOCl, four beakers with different solution were prepared, as 200 mL Bi(NO3)3·5H2O (2 mmol L-1) solution, 200 mL DI water, 200 mL KCl (Sigma-Aldrich, ≥99.0%) solution and 200 mL DI water. The BiVO4 films were placed into each solution for about 5 s in sequence. One process of the deposition could make one cycle of the BiOCl. The number of BiOCl cycle deposited on BiVO4 was adjusted as 5, 10, 20, 30 and 40. The obtained BiOCl/BiVO4 samples were cleaned with DI water and dried at room temperature for 20 h.

Fabrication of Bi2O4 on BiVO4 films: The Bi2O4 nano-particles were obtained from a simple alkali post-treatment process under the light illumination. The BiOCl/BiVO4 samples were placed in 0.01 M NaOH solution with the irradiation supplied by a 300 W xenon lamp for different time, as 2 min, 5 min, 10 min, 20 min, 30 min and 60 min. After that, the samples were washed with DI water several times and dried at room temperature for 20 h.

Fabrication of CQDs and related samples: CQDs were fabricated by the pyrolysis method. 0.75 g sucrose (Sigma-Aldrich, 99%) was dissolved in 30 mL DI water with stirring for 1 h. Then, the solution was transferred into a 40 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and heated to 180 °C for 5 h. The obtained CQDs powder was washed with DI water and dried at 60 °C. For preparing the CQDs loaded samples, photoanode samples were immersed in CQD solution (1 mg/mL) for 4 h, and washed with DI water afterwards and dried at 80 °C.

Photoelectrodeposition of Ni-FeOOH co-catalyst: First, FeOOH layer was photoelectrodeposited on photoanode samples in 0.1 M FeSO4 (FeSO4·7H2O, Sigma-Aldrich, ≥99.0%) solution at 0.25 V vs. Ag/AgCl (~45 mC/cm2). Then, NiOOH layer was photoelectrodeposited on the above samples in 0.1 M NiSO4 (NiSO4·6H2O, Sigma-Aldrich, ≥98%) solution at 0.11 V vs. Ag/AgCl (~22 mC/cm2). Finally, electrodeposition of additional NiOOH was conducted in 0.1 M NiSO4 solution at 1.2 V vs. Ag/AgCl for 1 min.

Photoelectrochemical measurement: PEC performance was measured by an IM6x electrochemical workstation (ZAHNER-Elektrik GmbH & Co., KG, Germany) in a typical three-electrode system, with BiVO4 samples as work electrodes, Pt foil as counter electrode, Ag/AgCl (Saturated KCl) electrode as reference electrode, and 0.5 M phosphate buffer solution with pH = 7 as the electrolyte. The solar light simulator (Newport solar simulator, model #94023A) was calibrated to 1 sun (100 mW cm−2) using a silicon reference solar cell equipped with KG-5 filter. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) Nyquist plots were obtained at 1.23 V vs. RHE with small AC amplitude of 10 mV in the frequency range of 0.1 to 10000 Hz. Mott-Schottky plots were collected by using frequency of 1 kHz and an Ac amplitude of 5 mV.

Characterization: The morphologies were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JEOL 7100F) with an accelerating voltage of 5 kV. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) were taken with a JEOL-2010 with an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. The crystal structure identification was determined by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Philips PW-1830 X-ray diffractometer, λ = 1.5406 Å, 298 K, Cu Kα radiation) at a scanning speed of 12.5° min−1 under operation conditions of 40 kV and 40 mA. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) and Kevin probe force microscopy (KPFM) were conducted on the Asylum Research MFP-3D-Bio AFM. A 32 W mercury lamp as the light source was used to investigate the contact potential difference (CPD) between the tip and the sample. In the KPFM mode, an AC bias at resonance frequency ω was used to explore the electrostatic force and an external DC voltage was applied to the cantilever to nullify the CPD and thus minimize the force. Therefore, the CPD could be determined by tuning the DC voltage. The chemical states of samples were measured by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, PerkinElmer model PHI 5600 XPS system, Mo Kα radiation). Ultraviolet photo-electron spectroscopy (UPS) was carried out with the photoelectrons collected along the surface normal of samples. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were measured using an UV–vis spectrophotometer with −10 V bias applied (PerkinElmer, model Lambda 20).

Theoretical Methods: All the theoretical work is based on first principle density functional theory, with Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) exchange–correlation functional, implemented in the Quantum Espresso package. The scalar-relativistic norm conserving pseudopotentials are used for all the elements. A 60 Ry kinetic energy cutoff and 2x2x2 K-Sampling is applied, with energy convergence criterion 10-5 eV per atom. Our optimized crystal structures parameters are very close to the experimental ones of BiVO4 [14] and Bi2O4 [15].

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: M. Ma and Z. Xing contributed equally to this work. The authors acknowledge the financial support from the RGC of Hong Kong (GRF Nos. 16312216), Shenzhen Peacock Plan (KQTD2016053015544057) and Nanshan Pilot Plan (LHTD20170001), National Natural Science Foundation of China (21905298, 21972006), Shenzhen Science and Technology Innovation Committee (JCYJ20190807164205542), and Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (2020A1515010342).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcat.2020.09.012.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
